item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As discussed, she’s yours. Can’t wait to see what you come up with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Give me the child, Spike.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We've got this, that seriously-don't-call-her-a-kitsune, and the space yacht of an ancient Chinese emperor. Three off the top of my head that are straight forward presentations of Asian myth as scp.

But we can't seem to hang on to any recognizable vampire or werewolf.

Is there something unique to Asian mythology that allows this? Maybe it's because most of the user base here are westerners; so the legends from another culture don't suffer from as much familiarity burn out?

Anyway, it's a good monster. The more I think about it, the really scary part is not what it does but that she honestly doesn't know she's a monster.

I picture her going to the clinic every day. Really caring about her patients and their soon to be newborns. Shocked and saddened when she hears yet another lady she worked with was attacked by the serial killer the papers dubbed "The Mommy Ripper". Thinking she has no appetite because of disgust, not realizing it's just that she had a big meal last night.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was all set to upvote this, but I downvoted after reading about the pregnant researcher. I loved the concept of the woman not knowing about her anomalous nature, I loved the fridge horror of her training as an obstetrics nurse, and the whole thing is very well written. But sending a pregnant woman to top off the vinegar basin is too much dumbfuckery. It was LOLFoundation worthy antics.

I get the need to show what happens if a pregnant woman is nearby, but there are so many nonstupid ways to show that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: its only called penanggal guys

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah sure. Give it a go!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think a rewrite is a good idea, with emphasis being placed on making this Penanggalan different from the traditional Penanggalan. I've always had a fondness for that particular myth (I just love how batshit insane it is), so I might be interested. However, I'd like to think on it for a while and see if I can come up with an original approach. If I think of a good idea then I might give this a shot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author here. If there is any interest from the community in someone taking a crack at doing a total re-write of this one, I'm interested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I had to downvote.

As others have pointed out before, this is really just a point-by-point retelling of the Penanggalan myth, without any original ideas (except maybe the addendum, but even that was problematic: why would anyone, much less a pregnant woman, enter a Penanggalan's chamber at night? If she's in charge of the creature, she would have known what could happen. It's also highly unlikely the Penanggalan would have had time to eat the entire baby + uterus before guards could respond.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love Penanggalans but this is just… a real-world myth typed up as an SCP article. Downvoted - there's nothing creative here, just a transcript of a real-world myth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the one hand, the "oh fuck, that poor woman", because presumably this was a pregnancy she wanted to get a kid out of and was decorating the room and all that (or maybe bringing my tortoise home has just gotten me into a nesting mood!). On the other…yeah, I'd like to think that given the risks to the fetus of working ANY job with scip interaction, she'd have been placed on desk duty until she had the kid. But that creep factor of "it ate her uterus, and the potential human growing in it" could still work if she didn't yet know she was pregnant.

Epic creep — find out you're pregnant when a scip rips your uterus out and eats it and the embryo inside it.

I didn't know the myth to be biased by the scip playing it straight, so +1 from me. Not that myths played straight really bother me, they got to be myths in the first place by tapping into our fears, so why not let that carry over to the Foundation unaltered?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well… sometimes I wonder if researchers are stupid or somehow mentally challenged; a pregnant woman entered the cell of a monster known for eating placenta? That must be a new low for common sense. The scip is okay, upvoted.

item.title: Barf.
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted. An excellent rendition of a traditional horror story.

The only thing that gets me is…why would they ever allow a pregnant female to work with it in the first place? If I were running the show I wouldn't allow any non-sterile female within a mile of the damned thing just in case they were pregnant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is truly nightmarish. I think this is the monster from the horror game EYES.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addendum SCP-1060.01A Following Incident 1060.01, no pregnant females are to be admitted into the containment chamber during SCP-1060's nighttime cycle.

If the addendum had ended there, the article as a whole would have been vastly improved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is exhibiting most of the issues with other "real myth" SCPs: there's nothing distinct about it from the original legend. With the 1000, at least, there was a story behind it. This one is just a repackaging of something that's already fairly well known.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's good.

It might be cool if she can't be aware of what she is for some reason, just to move away from it being pure legend. Maybe her brain just won't let her be traumatized like that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Researcher: "I must regretfully inform you, SCP-1060, that you… well… you're a floating mass of organs that likes to sit in vinegar."

SCP-1060: "YOU'RE A FLOATING MASS OF ORGANS THAT LIKES TO SIT IN VINEGAR!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, technically the theme was urban legends, and although some people did make a mythological entity, others SCP-ified different legends, and some went in other directions entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh… What kind of twisted bastard came up with that?

I think that's sufficient information to upvote this, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We don't redact and expunge because we don't want the researchers getting squicked out, we redact and expunge to hide information that isn't necessarily need-to-know for everyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a Penanggalan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two things to note: one, this was made for the 1000 contest, if I am not mistaken, and the entire theme of that contest was mythological creatures.

That, and suggesting that redactions be added is, 99% of the time, a very bad idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I don't feel that would be a very good redaction. There's nothing there that's on a 'need-to-know' basis or anything, so a redaction would secure a downvote from me.

As it is, however, I'm unfamiliar with the mythology, and as such I have no idea what's going on. So, I'll withhold a vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Too similar to the mythological thing, I think that it needs some twist to raise the interest in people that already knew the creature (althoug, being at +35, maybe not).

Worse, the word "subject" is used 22 times in just four paragraphs. I think this makes the flow of the document a bit clunky.

Finally, the last sentence could have benefitted from a "REDACTED" instead of the goriest parts.

I, for one, downvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In these interviews, subject has expressed a lack of awareness of her nighttime cycle physiology or activities and protests that she merely sleeps at night.

Step 1: Record SCP-1060 transforming.
Step 2: Show SCP-1060 footage.
Step 3: ????
Step 4: Profit.

In all seriousness, I'd like to see an experiment log where she's shown video footage of her transformation. Would the monster inside of her react to self-exposure? Is she already aware, and simply denying it? It'd definitely add awesome points to the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I live in the Southeast Asia region. Tonight, I happened to be taking a run on a path that strayed near a jungle. At one point during the run, a fucking twig or something fell from the tree right behind me. Immediately, this SCP flashed into my head and so I jumped like three feet and ran like, like something that runs goddam fast I don't know. Once I reached the relative safety of a bus stop, I collapsed onto a bench and resolved to upvote this SCP immediately when I got home, and also to hate you forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't really say that I find much of worth in this one… It really just copy-pastes the myth directly into the SCPs. The best mythical ones have seemed to at least add -something- to the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I guess that is different. I will remove my downvote because it was written pretty well. I had a draft that got shot down for being a recognizable creature of myth (not a vampire or werewolf) so I was judging it on that and it being in a published D&D book.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was posted in the context of a contest http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-376556 in which entrants were supposed to be based on folklore. I chose to do a straightforward version of this particular folk tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Fiend Folio printed in 1981 calls it a 'pĕnanggalan', and devotes a paragraph to the vinegar issue. It is a creepy monster, and not as widely known as others, but it is still a straightforward mythological creature. This entry follows the Wikipedia entry much more closely than the D&D version, being a living woman, a midwife, and having a diet of placental material. Downvoted for lack of a novel twist to the legend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: and like I said in the other place, the photo is poor quality—which reduces suspension of disbelief (since the thing is in containment and therefore available to be photographed), so I don't want to use it. I knew about this photo when I created the article as part of the Bright challenge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's from an Indonesian movie from the early eighties called Mystics in Bali.

The version I've seen you link in… looks like three different places so far on the wiki was stolen from a Cracked.com article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ma gog he's right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh gog how did I miss that we contained a frikkin' penanggalan

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Got Picture for this SCP

http://lagodaxnian.files.wordpress.com/2010/03/leak.jpg

I think its from an old movie or something. Some edit might be required

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My thinking was to keep it short and, er, sweet, and to let the creep from the actual legend speak for itself. As Moose correctly points out, it's a relatively straight translation of the legend into SCP form. I'd prefer to leave the rest of the details, including the retreival, to the imagination.

The one exception to this is that it's implicit that this woman apparently doesn't consciously know that there's anything unusual about her. And yet she trained as an obstetrics nurse…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this needs something more. I mean, it's got the weird image of the disembodied head flying around dangling organs, and then the thing about the pregnant women, but nothing else really. Like, it's unnerving a bit, but I think it's got some real creep potential here that isn't being tapped into.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needs a retrieval section, STAT. My god, that thing around pregnant women…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Exactly. It's an embryo.

Was an embryo. Now it's SCP food.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think he's talking about the embryo/fetus distinction. Wikipedia sez: "In humans, it is called an embryo until about eight weeks after fertilization (i.e. ten weeks Last Menstrual Period or LMP), and from then it is instead called a fetus."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We're gonna not start that here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First trimester = not a fetus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, I love this particular myth, so I was hoping to love this. But… it's only "alright". It's a pretty much completely straight port of the myth, well-written, but uninteresting. Good writing, but this really needs more. No vote for now, but I'll be checking back to see if it improves enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's called a penanggalan, a Malaysian vampire of sorts. I first encountered it in the AD&D Fiend Folio, where they called it a vargouille, and later looked up the origin to see how badly they'd mangled it (to their credit, they only missed the bit about the vinegar, and the fact that the penanggalan isn't undead as such).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me a little of SCP-742, but not a bunch. I've never heard of this legend, but frankly it's creepy as fuck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have never heard of this legend. It's uh, well. The addendum creeped me the hell out, so I guess I'm upvoting.

One wonders why that needed an incident to be added to the regulations, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've heard of this legend, and I was hoping somebody would be able to write it up well.
